speaker: SS
words: Were you still a little kid?

speaker: SS
words: ROY GLENN: I was one year old when we come to Juliaetta- where we landed.

speaker: SS
words: We stopped on American Ridge up therefor a few years and then we moved

speaker: SS
words: over on the Potlatch and I spent all the rest of my years- I'll be se

speaker: SS
words: venty-four years old New Year's Day- and we've been here ever since.

speaker: SS
words: Do you know why it was they picked this part of the country to come toout

speaker: SS
words: West?

speaker: RG
words: Well, some of my folks earlier, my mother's folks had come West and

speaker: RG
words: settled in American Ridge and so that's why we followed. Yes, it's a

speaker: RG
words: better country out here they figured. And so they'd been back, they'd

speaker: RG
words: been out here then they'd been back to North Carolina and told us a

speaker: RG
words: bout it, so we come West then.

speaker: SS
words: Who were they? Their name?

speaker: RG
words: Well, Woody. And then also, Martin Thomas, used to be the big banker

speaker: RG
words: here; he was related to my mother, too. So he had been back there, so

speaker: RG
words: we all come West. Let's see, my mother and my grandparents and my

speaker: RG
words: father, of course, - and who else came?- My mother's brother came, too.

speaker: RG
words: And we all arrived out here and we ve been here ever since. And my

speaker: RG
words: folks has passed on my uncles has all passed on.

speaker: SS
words: Did your father farm back there in Carolina?

speaker: RG
words: No, he worked in the woods most of the time, and he was atellin' me

speaker: RG
words: that he also worked in a- for the government. And he worked in a

speaker: RG
words: government distillery when they made whiskey in North Carolina. He

speaker: RG
words: worked for the government distillery. Now she was born on

speaker: RG
words: Fix Ridge, she's been here all her life.

speaker: SS
words: Oh, I didn't know. What was your maiden name?

speaker: SS
words: MABEL RICHARDSON GLENN: Richardson

speaker: RG
words: Richardson.

speaker: RG
words: My folks come here from Oregon in 1893 and they landed in Genesee,

speaker: RG
words: farmed there for a little while- a place near Genesee, then they moved

speaker: RG
words: and bought this on Fix Ridge and lived there ever since and I was born

speaker: RG
words: and raised there til met him and then I went to Texas Ridge.

speaker: SS
words: Was that land on Fix Ridge, was that naturally prairie land at the

speaker: SS
words: time, or was that cleared? Do you know? Where they were?

speaker: RG
words: Where?

speaker: SS
words: I was wondering was that land on Fix Ridge where they were was cleared

speaker: SS
words: land or just never had any trees on it.

speaker: RG
words: When they came, they bought it from people the name of Dew and it

speaker: RG
words: was cleared land but it had a great big orchard on it.

speaker: RG
words: He means was it prairie land or timber land?

speaker: RG
words: It was prairie land. Uh-huh.

speaker: SS
words: A lot of that Fix Ridge was, wasn't it?

speaker: RG
words: Yes, you bet.

speaker: RG
words: Bunchgrass they always called it.

speaker: RG
words: American Ridge was too until you get up towards Troy, you know. That

speaker: RG
words: was prairie land, and that's why old Martin Thomas homesteaded up on

speaker: RG
words: American Ridge, and you could have another 160 if you had a preemption

speaker: RG
words: of trees. So he set out five acres of trees to get another 160 with

speaker: RG
words: a homestead.

speaker: SS
words: Wasn't he about the biggest farmer there for a while?

speaker: RG
words: Oh, yeah. Then he was also president of the Kendrick State Bank here

speaker: RG
words: for years and years.

speaker: SS
words: How did he do so well? Is it just he was here earlier, or what was it

speaker: SS
words: that gave him the leg up?

speaker: RG
words: Yeah, he was just here early to start with. Yeah, that's where he got

speaker: RG
words: started. Yeah.

speaker: SS
words: Had your parents owned land at Genesee when they lived there?

speaker: RG
words: No.

speaker: SS
words: They were kind of looking for a place?

speaker: RG
words: Looking for a place. Then they traded— they had a place in Oregon

speaker: RG
words: and they made a trade somehow.

speaker: SS
words: Did your parents, when they came out, did they have much money to

speaker: SS
words: start out with, or did they just have to start from scratch here?

speaker: RG
words: Uh-huh. Yes, my dad worked on farms and he was also a carpenter and

speaker: RG
words: he done lots of carpenter work and stuff. That's how we survived for

speaker: RG
words: years and years. And of course, us family- they had a family; they

speaker: RG
words: was five boys and three girls in the family and they raised all of us.

speaker: RG
words: Yeah, we survived.

speaker: SS
words: Where was the farm then that you grew up on? Where was it?

speaker: RG
words: It was over here on the Potlatch Ridge and it wasn't too big a place.

speaker: RG
words: And we lived there on a small place then they rented a farm there and -

speaker: RG
words: a bigger farm and we grew up there and went to school and then after I

speaker: RG
words: got grown and all I got to thinking about going to the army and I didn't

speaker: RG
words: go to the army, so got to thinking about getting married and I met her

speaker: RG
words: and we been married for forty^eight years. And we lived on Texas Ridge

speaker: RG
words: up here. Our ranch is up on Texas Ridge. We go back and forth here.

speaker: RG
words: And the reason we're here is, we just had girls and they was in school

speaker: RG
words: and this was the school that we had to send 'em to, and there's bad

speaker: RG
words: years a getting in and out for the snow; lots of snow up there, so we

speaker: RG
words: got ahold of this place. They've all been through school and we're

speaker: RG
words: here by ourselves now, but we still— There was no school here, it was

speaker: RG
words: up on the hill in town so they bought this land. Now that land come

speaker: RG
words: off of this place before we bought the place, they got this land off

speaker: RG
words: of it - the school is here.

speaker: SS
words: Where on Potlatch Ridge? Were you near Leland?

speaker: RG
words: We was west of Leland, about four miles. Yeah, we was about four miles

speaker: RG
words: west of Leland.

speaker: SS
words: So what was it like when you were growing up here, when you were just

speaker: SS
words: a kid?

speaker: RG
words: Oh, it was quite different, I'll tell you. We had horses and we had

speaker: RG
words: all stock on the ranches, you know. It's about four miles to Leland,

speaker: RG
words: that used to be a little town up there and at one time we was just

speaker: RG
words: reading in the paper here not long ago that at one time six hundred

speaker: RG
words: people got their mail at Leland. That was just a small burg. You had

speaker: RG
words: good stores and blacksmith shop. Course, that was one of the great

speaker: RG
words: things in early day, was the blacksmith shop in a community, because

speaker: RG
words: thats the way you kept things arollin'. If you had a good smith in

speaker: RG
words: the country, you know, why, they done most of work for the

speaker: RG
words: machinery. Yes, and it was all horses at that time. We were just

speaker: RG
words: talking a few days ago; when they go to plow now, they don't make any

speaker: RG
words: difference whether it's dry or what it is, they can plow, but them days

speaker: RG
words: when you had the horses, you had to wait til you had moisture enough

speaker: RG
words: that the horses could pull it, you know. And the plows wasn't made

speaker: RG
words: to stay in the ground like they are now. All that was that way. Then

speaker: RG
words: our grain. There used to be a tram up on th€ ridge and they'd haul

speaker: RG
words: their grain over to this tram and it run by a cable and it had braces

speaker: RG
words: of buckets on it and they'd put their grain on these buckets in sacks

speaker: RG
words: and they'd take 'em off down at the bottom. Well, in Juliaetta, that

speaker: RG
words: was another tram, but that rttn on cars. They had two cars on the

speaker: RG
words: cables, and three^many a time and had three rails, but right in the

speaker: RG
words: middle of it there was four rails, so to pass they just one- went a

speaker: RG
words: little wider. Well, they'd pile your grain on that and run it down

speaker: RG
words: to Juliaetta. Put it on the train down there, they had a warehouse

speaker: RG
words: there. They didn't haul too much down the grade at that time, you know,

speaker: RG
words: therroads was rough and rocky to haul on, and if they could get it run

speaker: RG
words: down that way, why, that's the way they got a lot of their grain to

speaker: RG
words: the terminal down here.

speaker: SS
words: Was it really dangerous to use those roads for hauling at that time?

speaker: RG
words: Oh, well, no faster than you went it wasn't too much for the horses.

speaker: RG
words: Then as your old trucks that started in- the first of 'em was solid

speaker: RG
words: tires, you know. Well, a few of them come in and a few years later

speaker: RG
words: then they started agettin' small trucks. And we was just alookin' at

speaker: RG
words: one at Juliaetta- they got one down there at Juliaetta as a relic- i

speaker: RG
words: believe it was 19 and 22 or something like that, it was bought. That's

speaker: RG
words: about one of the first pneumatic tires they had, you know. And some

speaker: RG
words: body picked it up out of the junk and they had it arunnin' down

speaker: RG
words: there the other day. And to think there's stuff like that. We had onewell,

speaker: RG
words: when we got married, why, we, the folks and us had one and we

speaker: RG
words: an old-timer we got in the '30's and it's the same kind and you'd haul

speaker: RG
words: twenty-seven sacks of grain to town. That's a pretty g ood load! But

speaker: RG
words: it sure has changed that way. But everybody had horses and everybody

speaker: RG
words: had cows purtnear, and that was your survival. At that time it took

speaker: RG
words: about a fourth of 160 to feed the cows and the horses, what you raised

speaker: RG
words: on it, so that's what took up a lot of the stuff. nowdays it's

speaker: RG
words: all done by big machinery and that's put on the market. And of course

speaker: RG
words: the population is increased a lot, but still that's what makes your

speaker: RG
words: overproduction at the present time is because it's all put on the mar

speaker: RG
words: ket instead of like it used to be.

speaker: SS
words: What kind of crops would you be getting on the Potlatch Ridge in those

speaker: SS
words: earlier years? Was it a good yield then?

speaker: RG
words: Oh, yes. Now, one of the things they raised was beans. You know, we

speaker: RG
words: cultivated and hoed 'em you know, and that was a good crop, but on

speaker: RG
words: this ground you'd get a real good crop of grain after beans. You would

speaker: RG
words: get a lot better crop on beans ground than you would pea ground.

speaker: RG
words: ground was always clean. You had to cultivate beans, they was in

speaker: RG
words: rows, you know, and you'd cultivate them and then you'd also hoe 'em.

speaker: RG
words: And it took a lot of the young folkses time There was always kids

speaker: RG
words: wantin' to hoe beans, you know, they'd get a little money in the sum

speaker: RG
words: mer. That was a livelihood, you know for the family for the kids to

speaker: RG
words: get a little job a hoein' beans here and there.

speaker: RG
words: Then they'd have to cut 'em and then they'd have to pile 'em.

speaker: RG
words: Yeah, you'd cut 'em and then pile 'em. And when they maturedA

speaker: RG
words: haul 'em out in the wagons, you know, and put 'em in the thrashing

speaker: RG
words: machine. That took a lot of work. There was a lot of work-

speaker: RG
words: Then before the thrashing machine come they'd have a of horses

speaker: RG
words: and they'd tramp 'em out and you'd bring those beans in and these

speaker: RG
words: horses, have 'em on a flat surface, you see, and these horses would

speaker: RG
words: go around and tramp out these beans and you'd pick 'em up- pick up the

speaker: RG
words: straw and them it out then you'd pick up the beans.

speaker: RG
words: Yeah, they was a lot of ways that they done in them days.

speaker: SS
words: How come the beans? Were they better than peas at that time?

speaker: SS
words: Well, we had better ground after beans. But here come the peas along

speaker: SS
words: and they wasn't so much work to harvest them. And then our machinery

speaker: SS
words: changed. Here come the combines, you know. Well, in the beans, most

speaker: SS
words: of the time until the last few years you had to haul 'em and put 'em

speaker: SS
words: in a threshing machine you thrashed early grain in. They thrashed

speaker: SS
words: beans just like the grain, only in your machine you- about all you done

speaker: SS
words: - just your cyclinder down, so you didn't crack the beans

speaker: SS
words: and thrashed 'em with that same thrashing machine, If would take

speaker: RG
words: thirty and thirty-five bean thrashing. . ' like on the Ridge up

speaker: RG
words: there-

speaker: SS
words: Was that enough to make 'em give up beans?

speaker: RG
words: Well, seem like that they went to that more because they didn't have

speaker: RG
words: to have as much work to it and then these here combines, you know,

speaker: RG
words: these later ones, they could just- one man could cut a whole field of

speaker: RG
words: peas. The other way you had a lot of people working, and that's whyand

speaker: RG
words: I used to think that's that's what makes unemployment. The kids

speaker: RG
words: didn't have anything to do. Oh, yeah, they done a lot of that.

speaker: RG
words: What else did you kids do?

speaker: RG
words: Oh-Work. Was there anything else besides the beans?

speaker: RG
words: Not too much work besides beans and helping in the hay. If you was

speaker: RG
words: big enough to help in the hay, you'd have to help put up hay. And

speaker: RG
words: stuff like that was about the biggest thing there was for a kid to do.

speaker: RG
words: And milk cows. I started milking cows when I was four years old.

speaker: RG
words: Yes.

speaker: RG
words: There was all that work around the house. Just chores?

speaker: RG
words: Oh, yes, you bet. Everybody had chores. They pretty near everybody

speaker: RG
words: had hogs and they had chickens and they had cows, and you had chores

speaker: RG
words: to do and that, you know. And horses. Oh, yeah, it was a dif

speaker: RG
words: ferent altogether than the present day. We have big farmers now that

speaker: RG
words: hasn't got an animal on the place. It's different, we got animals and

speaker: RG
words: I guess we always will have because we are used to 'em and we like 'em.

speaker: RG
words: And maybe they don't make us too much money but then it's in the livlihood

speaker: RG
words: of people.

speaker: SS
words: Well, at that time though didn't your family depend on all that stuff?

speaker: SS
words: wasn't going and buying stuff at the store at all.

speaker: RG
words: Oh, no. You raised pretty near everything; your pork and your beef

speaker: RG
words: when you had some. And in them days though, they didn't have any

speaker: RG
words: coolers to keep this stuff in like you ' you know, your pork you

speaker: RG
words: pretty near had to cure it you see. Well, they had ways of curing pork

speaker: RG
words: and that was one of your main meats and then they'd always raise quite

speaker: RG
words: a few chickens and you could butcher a chicken any time you wanted fresh

speaker: RG
words: chicken;, fresh meat. And that was another thing that helped and they

speaker: RG
words: all had gardens. That was another thing which they don't do in the

speaker: RG
words: present day. That was their livlihood, you bet it was! Yes. After

speaker: RG
words: we was married in the Depression, milked cows and if you got sometimes,

speaker: RG
words: two dollars and thirty-five cents for five gallon can of cream, well,

speaker: RG
words: that wasn't very much, but your groceries wasn't much and you could

speaker: RG
words: still survive. I was just areadin' this little paper here; just read

speaker: RG
words: you some of the things that happened. They had something in here thatabout

speaker: RG
words: what the prices was at the later years- oh, shoot, I was just

speaker: RG
words: areadin' something in here just before you come in- Oh, yes, - what

speaker: RG
words: the heck was it? Blewitt's Cash Grocery advertised these breakfast

speaker: RG
words: items; Swift's bacon thirty-five cents a pound! Sperry hotcake and waf

speaker: RG
words: fle flour, ten pound bag fifty-five cents. New shipments of Albers

speaker: RG
words: rolled oats twenty-nine cents a package. Now that was in 1936, so

speaker: RG
words: you know long before that is when— I tell you, one thing I got to seethey

speaker: RG
words: built a new bank here a few years back. Well, the old bank was

speaker: RG
words: here, and when they had taken the books from the old bank, there's

speaker: RG
words: a business done Itik that old bank was just wrote every day. They

speaker: RG
words: had a big book about this big and this guy just wrote the business out.

speaker: RG
words: Well, I went down all this list, I happened to get to see this for

speaker: RG
words: 1910, that's when this was. My dad had ten dollars and seventyfive

speaker: RG
words: cents in the bank. A lot of people had three or four dollars.

speaker: RG
words: And the most on deposit that was on that book - his name was Albers

speaker: RG
words: and he lived on Bear Ridge and he had $335 in the bank. Now you can

speaker: RG
words: guess about how the A- well, one man could run the bank at that time

speaker: RG
words: in 1910, you know. And all this instead of- now down here they tell

speaker: RG
words: me they have to make six copies of everything that goes through this

speaker: RG
words: down here. Well, one man just wrote out the business, every day

speaker: RG
words: and then the next day it was a new page over here and starting what

speaker: RG
words: business was done and he'd just keep it wrote out. Now that's the way

speaker: RG
words: it was in 1910. So you know there wasn't too much money changing hands

speaker: RG
words: dont but they was quite a lot of tradin'. You trade somethin' you raised

speaker: RG
words: for somebody that had somethin' that you wanted and that's the way they

speaker: RG
words: go about the business, a lot of it.

speaker: SS
words: You mean, it was just trading between the farmers?

speaker: RG
words: Yeah, a lot of the stuff would be just kind of traded. That's why they

speaker: RG
words: survived, you know, because they didn't have money to buy with but probably this fellow

speaker: RG
words: had something you wanted, and if you had something

speaker: RG
words: that you could interest him in.it was a trade. But in town, stuff was

speaker: RG
words: so cheap in them years that,well, that was 1910. Now 1906 and '07 was

speaker: RG
words: a lot- I saw a note that a fellow had to give; he bought three dollars

speaker: RG
words: worth of spuds from a neighbor and he didn't have ncjthing he wanted,

speaker: RG
words: so he give him a note for three hundred pounds of spuds and he paid

speaker: RG
words: him a dollar a year for the spuds, but he had to pay twelve percent in

speaker: RG
words: terest on thatl Now that's the way it was in the early days here.

speaker: RG
words: But the land- well, nobody was in a hurry about buyin' somebody else's

speaker: RG
words: land like it is now. If you had some land and that was your home, that's

speaker: RG
words: just about all you wanted; if you had a place to live. But now it's

speaker: RG
words: gone the other way. But I was just gonna tell you, they was a lot of

speaker: RG
words: trouble in the land in them days though. If you owned some land, no

speaker: RG
words: body was gonna get it away from ya, unless they had some excuse. I

speaker: RG
words: was just thinkin'; there was two men killed just to establish a line

speaker: RG
words: fence. Two men got in an argument and one went and killed the other

speaker: RG
words: and then the other'n got scared and hung hisself and it said in the

speaker: RG
words: paper- that was in 1906 I think, they tell me, and I was just a kid,

speaker: RG
words: and they said in the records that they was two men lost their lives

speaker: RG
words: to establish a line fence in Idaho! Well, that was in the national,

speaker: RG
words: you know.

speaker: SS
words: Was this on Texas Ridge?

speaker: RG
words: That's right.

speaker: SS
words: What happened to the man that killed the other guy? First he shot the

speaker: SS
words: other guy and then he hung himself?

speaker: RG
words: Yeah. He run off in the canyon to get away from the law and he star

speaker: RG
words: ved out. In about three days he come to a neighbors house and the nei

speaker: RG
words: ghbor was gone and he went in to get something to eat. Well, the nei

speaker: RG
words: ghbor come while he was there. Now that was before my time, -^o remem

speaker: RG
words: ber much, but I've been in this house where he hung hisself and they

speaker: RG
words: told me all about it. And so the man that lived there said he had to

speaker: RG
words: report it to the officers that he'd come and so he didn't want him to

speaker: RG
words: but in this house; it was a fine log house and in the upstairs they'd

speaker: RG
words: had long logs went through but they'd never- the floor was just

speaker: RG
words: about half of the house. They had beds up there and they had a good

speaker: RG
words: stairway and while this fellow went to get the sheriff, why, he goes

speaker: RG
words: up this stairway where the floor was, you know, and put a rope around

speaker: RG
words: one of these big old rafters and then he jumped off of there and when

speaker: RG
words: they come back, why, he was ahangin' there dead, so they both lost their

speaker: RG
words: lives. So then they got the court, and they sent surveyors out and

speaker: RG
words: they surveyed this fence and set it on the line and I farmed this place

speaker: RG
words: for a long time, where this fence was there, you know. And it said in

speaker: RG
words: the records there thatlife-to establish a line fence

speaker: RG
words: cost two lives in Idaho.

speaker: SS
words: I guess the land really meant something.

speaker: RG
words: Oh, it did in a way. It was yours and you wasn't going to get a foot

speaker: RG
words: of it! Well, we had neighbors that after we went to Texas Ridge that

speaker: RG
words: had a Devil's Lane- now you know what that is? And fence it; your fence

speaker: RG
words: was here and his was here, they wouldn't join fences with you. But

speaker: RG
words: this land in between there; each one looked after his fence and they

speaker: RG
words: called that the Devil's Lane. Because we had some of that on our ridge

speaker: RG
words: after we was married there; still had a Devil's Lane fence. So you

speaker: RG
words: can see right there that they had a hard time with their la nd and no

speaker: RG
words: body was going to get any for nothing.

speaker: SS
words: If you had a fence like that, would that just mean you didn't trust

speaker: SS
words: your neighbor to keep on the line? Right?

speaker: RG
words: I guess that's what it'd be. Yeah, he didn't want him- you didn't

speaker: RG
words: want him on your place, either. And that was the line that he put out-

speaker: RG
words: Yeah, they had that.

speaker: SS
words: Well, on that Devil's Lane; did that just get all brushy?

speaker: RG
words: Oh, yeah.

speaker: SS
words: You couldn't walk through it?

speaker: RG
words: Nobody'd walk through it, they didn't want to be on it, you know. And

speaker: RG
words: it'd just be a little strip along there.

speaker: SS
words: How wide maybe?

speaker: RG
words: oh, this wide. But they wouldn't put fences with the other fellow. Now

speaker: RG
words: days, you know, it's "You take so much and I'll take so much." But

speaker: RG
words: that's the way it was in the early days. It happened that way lots of

speaker: RG
words: different places, that they had Devil's Lanes.

speaker: SS
words: They figured, too, maybe that the other guy wouldn't keep up his fence.

speaker: RG
words: Well, not toomuch of that. They'd both have pretty good fence, But

speaker: RG
words: most neighbors got along good. Once in a while they had trouble.

speaker: SS
words: Was it hard to get land at that time? I mean, if you wanted to farm

speaker: SS
words: like you say you had a little place-

speaker: RG
words: No, it wasn't hard if you had some money. There was fellows that

speaker: RG
words: owned a lot of land they got it in different transactions, you know,

speaker: RG
words: and it was for rent. A lot of land was rented; an awful lot of land

speaker: RG
words: in them days was rented. Some farmer come along- we used to rent bean

speaker: RG
words: ground. If you was a young fellow, you could- if you had any horses

speaker: RG
words: you could plow or work, you could rant bean ground from some fellow

speaker: RG
words: on shares and then he'd take it and plant it to wheat, you know, after

speaker: RG
words: wards. You'd make a little money in the summer. A young feller could

speaker: RG
words: get a little money out of it. And that's the way some of 'em got a lit

speaker: RG
words: tle start. If you happened to hit a year that the price was pretty

speaker: RG
words: do good and the beans were pretty good, you might pretty good on it.

speaker: RG
words: And they was lots of little old lumbermills around here them days, and

speaker: RG
words: they could always- when you got old enough to work at that you could

speaker: RG
words: pick up a little job. Yeah, they was a lot of little mills, lumbermills

speaker: RG
words: all over the country.

speaker: SS
words: But that wouldn't be the year around or nothing. That'd just be a

speaker: SS
words: seasonal thing? Right?

speaker: RG
words: Yeah. In the summer. And in the winter sometimes they cut logs and

speaker: RG
words: hauled em on to the mill with the horses. And them days you

speaker: RG
words: couldn't haul very far, you know, with the horses. But when the snow

speaker: RG
words: was on, sleighing, well, that was when it was a good time to haul a

speaker: RG
words: few logs to the sawmill, you know.

speaker: SS
words: Was there quite a few mills around here, too?

speaker: RG
words: Oh, yeah.They was a lot of little mills. Yeah, even after we was mar

speaker: RG
words: ried they was little mills up on our ridge all around. Get a atch

speaker: RG
words: of timber and set up a little sawmill and saw the lumber. Yeah. They

speaker: RG
words: used to be a sawmill right the other side of this schoolhouse here for

speaker: RG
words: two-three years, wasn't they?

speaker: RG
words: Yeah.

speaker: RG
words: And then down below town here where this shop is there, that used to

speaker: RG
words: be a sawmill. And then up on Cedar Ridge here, they used to be saw

speaker: RG
words: mills and back of Leland and Southwick country, you know, they been a

speaker: RG
words: lot of sawmills there and not too many years back. But that land's all

speaker: RG
words: cleared up pretty near now. They come in here with these big tractors,

speaker: RG
words: you know, and they soon take the timber off n there and^they clear the

speaker: RG
words: land and start farming.

speaker: SS
words: Would the samwill be owned by just one man?

speaker: RG
words: Sometimes it would, yeah.

speaker: SS
words: And he'd just move it around from place to place?

speaker: RG
words: Well, he'd usually set one in where there's timber. Now on Cedar Creek

speaker: RG
words: up here, Mr. Darby had a sawmill- he's passed on now- but that was there

speaker: RG
words: for years. And it was right in a country where they's lots of timber

speaker: RG
words: and lots of people worked for him. And a lot of times he'd saw in the

speaker: RG
words: winter, too if it wasn't too cold. You know that was the problem. They

speaker: RG
words: just haA roofs over 'em, you know. Yeah, they done a lot of sawin'.

speaker: RG
words: But then they d saw the logs a lot in the winter and haul 'em

speaker: RG
words: the mill on a sled so they'd have 'em for the spring.

speaker: SS
words: He'd have a crew of men that would stay right there?

speaker: RG
words: Yup. And the neighbors around there'd work a lot, you know. There'd

speaker: RG
words: be a house here and a cabin over there and somethin'. Survival wasthem

speaker: RG
words: days, seemed like was about all they thought about. Nobody cared

speaker: RG
words: too much about being rich. It was survival. Well, a lot of these old

speaker: RG
words: canyon places that nobo^ thinks about livin (in)-

speaker: RG
words: SIDE B

speaker: SS
words: crews and all that, when you were growing up.

speaker: RG
words: Oh, yeah.

speaker: SS
words: Would that be the main way that some people would make money? For

speaker: SS
words: a year?

speaker: RG
words: Yup. They'd go out and harvest. See, you had to cut this grain,

speaker: RG
words: shock it, and then you had to run it through the machine, well, you

speaker: RG
words: didn't have too many machines and it took a long time. If you had a

speaker: RG
words: job with a machine it'd last a long time; forty days or sometimes

speaker: RG
words: longer than that. And they'd pay you for that and then if you'd

speaker: RG
words: home you'd have a garden and stuff, and so, that's the way they'd make

speaker: RG
words: enough money to get through on.

speaker: SS
words: So that might be for some of these, for some folks, that would maybe

speaker: SS
words: be the only real employment that they would have?

speaker: RG
words: That's right.

speaker: SS
words: They could still get by? Still survive on that?

speaker: RG
words: And raise a family. But it didn't cost much them days. We did have

speaker: RG
words: an old catalogue here once. What was that 1906 or something like that

speaker: RG
words: wasn't it? You got a man's suit of clothes for three dollars and a

speaker: RG
words: half!

speaker: SS
words: Would guys raising a family like that, would they be thought of as

speaker: SS
words: lacking ambition if they didn't have anything but a job?

speaker: RG
words: No, no, that was the only way they survived and they was good workers,

speaker: RG
words: too, and stuff like that. Some of our best men worked like that. Now,

speaker: RG
words: in town here where the bank is, there is no trees there at that time

speaker: RG
words: and that was - in the winter people d have some grain at home,where

speaker: RG
words: they thrashed and they'd keep it, you know. But in the winter sleig

speaker: RG
words: hing was gettin' good and they'd haul it to town on the sled. And

speaker: RG
words: everybody'd bring feed for their horses and they'd feed there in that

speaker: RG
words: lot, you know, feed their horses and they'd bring their own lunch,

speaker: RG
words: usually and that's where they'd spend the noonhour. And then from

speaker: RG
words: this ridge and that ridge and all, and then they'd start home. And

speaker: RG
words: everybody knew everybody. That's the way it turned out.

speaker: SS
words: So they visited? They did a lot of visiting in town here?

speaker: RG
words: Oh, yeah. And while their horses was eatin' and all, they'd eat their

speaker: RG
words: lunch. Everybody knew thatAhe was a farmer and knew pretty near the

speaker: RG
words: other feller, you know. And that's the way it turned out.

speaker: SS
words: Was there places to go inside, to get warm?

speaker: RG
words: Oh, yeah. Yeah, they had places. They had poolhalls and stuff, you

speaker: RG
words: know, they could go and set in there. Yup. Kendrick burned up in 1904.

speaker: RG
words: It was mostly wooden buildings and in 1904 they had a big fire in the

speaker: RG
words: summer and burned it up and all rebuilt after that. Built

speaker: RG
words: in different ways. That's why they got a lot of brick buildings. They

speaker: RG
words: used to have a brick kiln right down here across the bridge by town.

speaker: RG
words: And they made up all most the brick right here in town. Took the old

speaker: RG
words: clay off the hillside and put it through that and them places in

speaker: RG
words: town- most all homemade brick.

speaker: SS
words: I didn't know that.

speaker: RG
words: Yeah. Yeah.

speaker: SS
words: Who's place was that? That brick plant?

speaker: RG
words: Joe Fricktell owned it. And old guy by the name of Joe Fricktell run

speaker: RG
words: the plant.

speaker: SS
words: Pretty darn good brick.

speaker: RG
words: Oh, yes. He made good bricks here. Made a lot of buildings. Lot of

speaker: RG
words: these houses around here, that's where they's from and stuff.

speaker: SS
words: He had that plant before the fire started? Before the fire?

speaker: RG
words: He did, before the fire.

speaker: SS
words: And all of a sudden he had a lot of business.

speaker: RG
words: Oh, gosh, yes. He couldn't take care of the business, some of it had

speaker: RG
words: to be brought in, you know. They couldn't get enough here.

speaker: SS
words: Do you think it very long for them to get the town built the way it

speaker: SS
words: is now after that fire?

speaker: RG
words: Not too long. Everybody that was in business was startin's back to

speaker: RG
words: work on it, you know, and get things up. We had just come here, you

speaker: RG
words: know, I was tellin' you, and this old barn right up here is on our

speaker: RG
words: place and my dad, when we come to the country up - he got a job on a

speaker: RG
words: farm out here on American Ridge. Well, that fella, he run a farm and

speaker: RG
words: he also had the drayline in Kendrick. Everything was shipped in by

speaker: RG
words: rail and then delivered around town with a team, you know. Well, they

speaker: RG
words: used to keep their horses up in this barn. And my dad'd work on the

speaker: RG
words: farm and when they was quite a bit of business to deliver, why they'd

speaker: RG
words: have him come down here and help deliver stuff for the town. No, it

speaker: RG
words: soon started to grow right up again just after the fire. It wasn't

speaker: RG
words: too long til it was up and they built it up, the buildings, and I can

speaker: RG
words: recall the celebration. We went to the celebration and they didn't

speaker: RG
words: have any trees in town for shade, so they went over on the hilsdde,

speaker: RG
words: you know there's a lot of firs and stuff over there, and they'd take

speaker: RG
words: barrels and they'd cut off a tree, oh ten feet high or fifteen, maybe,

speaker: RG
words: and set it in there and then pile rocks in that barrel and that was

speaker: RG
words: the shade they had in town in the early days. That's the way they'd

speaker: RG
words: celebrate. It was awful hot down here and of course, the streets wasin

speaker: RG
words: the summer they was all dusty and in the winter it was all mud and

speaker: RG
words: stuff.

speaker: SS
words: Was this celebration you're talking about for the Fourth of July?

speaker: RG
words: That's right. That was a big thing here in them days. But now they

speaker: RG
words: just pretty near forget it. Oh, yes, if we got to go to the Fourth

speaker: RG
words: of July, that was a big day, you know it? Farmers would all go. That

speaker: RG
words: was the one day all figured on going And they'd all go see

speaker: RG
words: the neighbors and have a ballgame and horse races once in a while, and

speaker: RG
words: stuff like that. That was the entertainment. If you had twenty-five

speaker: RG
words: cents you could do pretty good at the celebration. Cost you a nickle

speaker: RG
words: for pretty near anything- you could get for a nickle!

speaker: SS
words: Does that mean that on Fourth of July, that the town would be mobbed

speaker: SS
words: with people from all over these ridges?

speaker: RG
words: Yes. Wherever they celebrated. Now sometimes they didn't celebrate

speaker: RG
words: in town, they'd celebrate in some place where there was a lot of shade

speaker: RG
words: trees or something. Now up there at Leland they didn't celebrate in

speaker: RG
words: town. But the trees is still there, a lot of 'em, they called that

speaker: RG
words: the park and they'd all meet in there and have a big day, but Kendrick

speaker: RG
words: Juliaetta would celebrate, that's the only places they had to be

speaker: RG
words: would be right in town.

speaker: SS
words: You know that fire there; did many people leave Kendrick, do you know?

speaker: RG
words: Not too many. Not too many.

speaker: SS
words: Most all the merchants went back into business?

speaker: RG
words: That's right. And then different ones- it was startin' a pretty good

speaker: RG
words: business town. Aba you know, the railroad had been coming through here

speaker: RG
words: and of course, earlier days they took in most of the stuff from Pierce

speaker: RG
words: come out of Kendrick when the mining days was on, see. Gold mine. And

speaker: RG
words: they come out- a lot of it out of Kendrick, so they'd been adoin' pret

speaker: RG
words: ty good just before the fire a while. I think the railroad come through

speaker: RG
words: here in 1891. Well, then, most of the stuff come from Moscow

speaker: RG
words: and then the railroad come of course, that opened up that whole country up north.

speaker: RG
words: They didn't have any railroad up the Clearwater, see,

speaker: RG
words: it all come from out of here. So that was quite a thing.

speaker: RG
words: In the early days they always told us about- this was a big part of

speaker: RG
words: the town, too. And the residences would be around here and they had

speaker: RG
words: wooden sidewalks. They didn't have this road there them days. It

speaker: RG
words: flooded all of 'em out.

speaker: RG
words: Yes this used to be- when the timber was back in the mountains here,

speaker: RG
words: this river would run, well, golly, it run big all summer. The snow

speaker: RG
words: would never- since the timber's cut off, it purt near dries up, you

speaker: RG
words: know, it gets awful low in the summer. But anyway, this road here

speaker: RG
words: was all level with this part here and they told us that the first

speaker: RG
words: bank that they had was an old time bank right here, and they had saloons

speaker: RG
words: here and when it was a floodin this'd flood so they built the side

speaker: RG
words: walks and they put 'em up on stilts; away up. And finally they had

speaker: RG
words: to put bannisters on 'em because the fellas'd get drunk when they's

speaker: RG
words: gambling here- they gambled here- and they'd fall into the river. So

speaker: RG
words: they had to put bannisters on 'em. Oh, we've had fellas here that told

speaker: RG
words: us in the early days, that this was the town when he was here.

speaker: SS
words: No kidding!

speaker: RG
words: The first stores was built here and then going out to Pierce, there's

speaker: RG
words: a little creek goes up here, and they just had a pack trail up that

speaker: RG
words: creek to go to Pierce. And that's the way they survived in here. Now

speaker: RG
words: these fellas had some farms up here but everything was fed up

speaker: RG
words: pretty near and utilized there you know, til they got this tram built

speaker: RG
words: to get it down here. They didn't have no road that you could get up

speaker: RG
words: and down with hardly anything at that early day.

speaker: SS
words: Then from here to Pierce was pack trail?

speaker: RG
words: Yeah.

speaker: SS
words: Would this be in the '90s?

speaker: RG
words: Yeah. And up then, even after the '90's. Up until, oh, 1903 and '04

speaker: RG
words: and '06, along there, they still had the packtrails. See- Pierce waswell,

speaker: RG
words: there was really no road to it much, only packtrails untilthey

speaker: RG
words: finally built just some old wagon wagons; cut 'em out of the

speaker: RG
words: brush and then the sawmill went in. And then that's when they build

speaker: RG
words: the town as they made their own lumber. And there's old buildings in

speaker: RG
words: this country now that's built out of just the old rough lumber, see,

speaker: RG
words: and then they covered it over. That's the way most of the towns was

speaker: RG
words: them days.

speaker: SS
words: Well, I've heard that going from Peck around in that country, some peo

speaker: SS
words: ple before the railroad got up in there would come to Kendrick for

speaker: SS
words: their supplies.

speaker: RG
words: Oh, yeah. Oh, they did. The railroad was here and a lot of people

speaker: RG
words: come here, you bet. All this back country come in from here.

speaker: SS
words: All the way to Elk River, I'm sure.

speaker: RG
words: Oh, yeah, because this was the only way to get in.

speaker: SS
words: Well, say like your family; did they trade mostly in Kendrick? Or did

speaker: SS
words: they trade at Leland?

speaker: RG
words: Well, we traded mostly at Leland at the store. But if we had grain

speaker: RG
words: or stock; now we used to sell hogs, you know, and ship 'em from

speaker: RG
words: here, stuff like that, we'd have to bring them to Kendrick to get 'em

speaker: RG
words: out of here. But at the store- they had a freighter, they called 'em,

speaker: RG
words: and they had wagons and they'd get this stuff off of the rail down here

speaker: RG
words: at the depot and they'd take it to Leland, to the store there. And 4\tn

speaker: RG
words: that whole community traded there. So they didn't come to Kendrick too

speaker: RG
words: much, you know,they done their tradin', what we called at home, pretty

speaker: RG
words: near. We called that home them days. They had a big flour mill at

speaker: RG
words: one time beyond Leland. And made flour. And when the roads got so

speaker: RG
words: they could haul it, why, they'd store it and they'd ship it out of the

speaker: RG
words: country, you know, to other places. Oh, they had a mammoth big flour

speaker: RG
words: mill. And they had storage for quite a little grain. And they'd buy

speaker: RG
words: the grain and take it in there and clean it. You had to wash it them

speaker: RG
words: days, too, you know, like they do now. And so they had a big well

speaker: RG
words: fixed up so they could have plenty of water and they'd wash it. Then

speaker: RG
words: they had lots of feed there. They'd take your grain there and have

speaker: RG
words: your feed ground for your stock, you know. Then you'd take it back to

speaker: RG
words: the ranch. This big mill had a feed grinder, too, in the flour mill.

speaker: SS
words: They employ many people there?

speaker: RG
words: Not too many. Eight or ten I imagine. But I can remember the old man's

speaker: RG
words: name. The Old Man Zeigler was the flour mill maker. And old, old,

speaker: RG
words: fella. And he'd look after the flour department, and you'd see him

speaker: RG
words: sack the flour. In them days it didn't come out in paper sacks, it

speaker: RG
words: come out in muslin sacks, you know, white ones.

speaker: SS
words: Was there any waterpower for that mill?

speaker: RG
words: It was steam.

speaker: SS
words: Steam!

speaker: RG
words: Yeah. It was steam. Yeah. Now most of these sawmills back here was

speaker: RG
words: steam, too. They'd get in a big steam boiler and some motors. Well,

speaker: RG
words: it ain't been long since the old steam boiler set up this little creek

speaker: RG
words: up here for years and years. The old boiler set there and the mill

speaker: RG
words: was closed and gone but the old boiler set there for years.

speaker: SS
words: What did your family buy, mostly during the year? What did they need

speaker: SS
words: to buy? Did you do catalogue order for your clothes and stuff?

speaker: RG
words: Yes. Sears  Roebuck; you could order. But anyway shoes and clothes.

speaker: RG
words: We raised our own vegetables and stuff, you know, and your own gar

speaker: RG
words: den, and you had your own meat, most all the time, and stuff like tha t.

speaker: RG
words: you just had to buy, oh, your stuff that you cooked with, your salt

speaker: RG
words: and your stuff like that was the main thing. And different stuff

speaker: RG
words: for the table. Didn't buy too much for the table, you had spuds and

speaker: RG
words: you had beans and you had your meat and your stuff like that. You

speaker: RG
words: had practically all of it for the year, you know. And then you had to

speaker: RG
words: live to that. We've talked about it a lot, what they have to eat at

speaker: RG
words: the present day and then we'd say, "Well, we had so-and-so when we

speaker: RG
words: was kids." "Oh well, they don't eat that much anymore." "Well,"I

speaker: RG
words: said, "we done awful well. We growed up on it anyway." But eating

speaker: RG
words: has changed a whole lot, We were just talkin' about- now

speaker: RG
words: my wife here's quite a gardener, and that's our livilihood now. We

speaker: RG
words: just eat a lot of that stuff, you know, from the garden. Our store

speaker: RG
words: bill is never very high because of fancy stuff. We got our own deepfreeze

speaker: RG
words: and we have our own meat, you know, and that's something you

speaker: RG
words: didn't have before. You had to cure.meat and keep it in someplace

speaker: RG
words: cool or something, you know, and it was a problem. You couldn't have

speaker: RG
words: fresh meat hardly. But now in later years when I was a kid before I

speaker: RG
words: could get old enough to harvest, they had a meat market here in town

speaker: RG
words: and they had a buggy with a top on it and a box on the back and they'd

speaker: RG
words: put fresh meat in there. And of course, everybody had a icehouse, and

speaker: RG
words: up they put ice with sawdust, you know for the year, and they put a chunk

speaker: RG
words: of ice in there for this meat. If you was athrashing they'd take you

speaker: RG
words: out some meat and leave it at the thrashing- where they was thrashing.

speaker: RG
words: Mr. Long down here is still alive that used to drive that wagon and

speaker: RG
words: take meat all over the country. Different days he'd be at different

speaker: RG
words: to give em some meat, you know. Well, everybody had a icehouse,

speaker: RG
words: Her folks had a icehouse, or her brother, but in town here, every store

speaker: RG
words: pretty near had a icehouse. Well, they'd cut it off of the river here

speaker: RG
words: in the winter, you know and put it in there. And that is what they

speaker: RG
words: had to keep their stuff with, is ice.

speaker: SS
words: On the ridges, where did they get their ice from?

speaker: SS
words: Well, some of 'em had ponds. Had ponds built and they'd get it off a

speaker: SS
words: that.

speaker: RG
words: Then they'd come to the river.

speaker: RG
words: Yeah . In the winter they could come down here and haul it out on a

speaker: RG
words: bobsled. Oh, some of 'em had big icehouses here in town and they'd

speaker: RG
words: sell you ice. You could come in and you could get ice pretty near any

speaker: RG
words: time, couldn't you?

speaker: RG
words: Uh-huh.

speaker: SS
words: You know, you were talking about the threshing a little bit, and I

speaker: SS
words: was thinking, those crews, were they almost all local men, or did- was

speaker: SS
words: it a lot of guys who would come in?

speaker: RG
words: Well, they had some guys, but they was all pretty near local fellas

speaker: RG
words: in the community. They'd be a few outsiders, but they'd be local fel

speaker: RG
words: las pretty much.

speaker: SS
words: Was that a time when you could have a lot of socialability?

speaker: RG
words: Oh, yeah. There was something going on all the time, and it didn't

speaker: RG
words: cost you like it does now.

speaker: SS
words: I was thinking when you were threshing; because I was thinking if every

speaker: SS
words: body was getting together kind of to thresh lots of times- or maybe

speaker: SS
words: there wasn't lots of time, with ali the hard work-

speaker: RG
words: No, there wasn't a lot of time.

speaker: RG
words: I don't know if you've ever seen an old threshing machine or not.

speaker: SS
words: I've seen pictures, but that one is a beauty. That's got the whole

speaker: SS
words: works, doesn't it? That's really something. Where was that?

speaker: SS
words: That's on Fix Ridge. And here's one of my brothers and here's one of

speaker: SS
words: my brothers. See, I was the youngest of the family. And this one is

speaker: SS
words: nineteen years old*and this one's sixteen. And this fellow's even

speaker: SS
words: older than I. I wasn't very old at that time. And see the old steam

speaker: SS
words: engine.

speaker: SS
words: That is a beautiful picture. Just to have the whole thing laid out

speaker: SS
words: like that is really great.

speaker: RG
words: That shows you the horses, four horses on a wagon and here's your bun

speaker: RG
words: dles in there. And the pitchers, they would stay out in the field and

speaker: RG
words: these fellers would haul into the threshing machine and throw it over

speaker: RG
words: into the feeder, and then it would go through. It don't show the sack

speaker: RG
words: pile there, does it?

speaker: SS
words: Well, you can see it. And you can see the spout. And that's the

speaker: SS
words: straw pile.

speaker: RG
words: And that's the straw pile and that's what we used for our horses and

speaker: RG
words: cattle in the wintertime to feed.

speaker: SS
words: The straw pile?

speaker: RG
words: Uh-huh. A lot of it. They'd run out to the straw piles.

speaker: RG
words: They'd be lots of chaff and stuff in that, see, and they'd all go in

speaker: RG
words: the straw pile. And they'd come through pretty good sometimes.

speaker: RG
words: On our farm, we had always raised a lot of corn and we cut this here

speaker: RG
words: corn and we'd feed 'em this corn fodder along with this, straw. And

speaker: RG
words: shuck the corn, feed that to the pigs and fatten them out. (Chuckles)

speaker: SS
words: How hard was the work on that- at that time of the year? How hard did

speaker: SS
words: the men work? Did they work real long days?

speaker: RG
words: Oh, boy they would put in from daylight til dark.

speaker: RG
words: In the summertime. They figured on being in the field and a going at

speaker: RG
words: six o'clock in the morning. And you had these horses to get ready to

speaker: RG
words: go, you know.

speaker: RG
words: Now here's what they called the header. Now this was big- this was a

speaker: RG
words: long time before this. They'd go along and cut the heads off and put

speaker: RG
words: 'em in this wagon and then they'd take 'em to the machine.

speaker: SS
words: Yup, so six o'clock. Did they get out in the field by six?

speaker: RG
words: Oh, yes, and then they'd work til seven o'clock in the evening.

speaker: RG
words: There's another picture. I started out when I was twelve years old

speaker: RG
words: working at a cookshack.

speaker: SS
words: When you were twelve?

speaker: RG
words: When I was twelve.

speaker: SS
words: What did you do in a cookshack at that age?

speaker: SS
words: M

speaker: RG
words: I cooked! Cooking for the men. Now this is my sister and my cousin,

speaker: RG

speaker: SS
words: When you say tile, would that help?

speaker: RG
words: Well, you put tile in the ground that if you've got a wet place over

speaker: RG
words: here that is slow about farming. It's a four inch plastic pipe and

speaker: RG
words: you dig a trench down and lay that down about this deep, you know, and

speaker: RG
words: that'll drain that water out so you won't have a mud hole there in the

speaker: RG
words: summer. And I tiled some of that. They have a machine to dig this

speaker: RG
words: with that just goes like this and digs it down and there's a auger

speaker: RG
words: here and it augers it out each way. And it'll dig a ditch this deep

speaker: RG
words: just pretty near as fast as you can walk. Well, then when this tile

speaker: RG
words: just folds right in there and then you push the dirt back over it.

speaker: RG
words: But this tile has got creases in it and little holes and the water can

speaker: RG
words: go in it anyplace and if it's got a slope it'll drain out, you know,

speaker: RG

speaker: RG

speaker: RG
words: on them low places a long time and then that'd be mud way up when

speaker: RG
words: you'd like to be a farmin'. Well, this way, it helps drain it out

speaker: RG
words: so that you can farm better.
